docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Trey Documentation Local-first AI engineering operations, documented end to end.

Learn how Trey coordinates agents, runners, runtimes, git worktrees, live telemetry, daemon-verified safety gates, and human approval before code reaches shared systems.

Get Started Learn Concepts RUN MONITOR 
$ trey run verify-security
[+] Claimed task-482 (high priority)
[+] Checked out worktree in 140ms
[+] Running static verification...
[+] Verification successful.
[+] Committing patch and creating PR.
[+] Session completed successfully.

Create agents, assign scopes, manage prompts, and configure approval defaults.

Explore 18 topics → DAEMON AND RUNNERS Daemon and Runners

Explore 13 topics → DEPLOYMENT AND OPERATIONS Deployment and Operations

Explore 10 topics → GETTING STARTED Getting Started

Explore 12 topics → PRODUCT ROADMAP Product Roadmap

Explore 11 topics → RUNTIME ADAPTERS Runtime Adapters

Explore 11 topics → SAFETY AND GOVERNANCE Safety and Governance

Manage and audit active CLI and web console sessions.

Run deterministic checks owned by the daemon, not the model response.

Explore 11 topics → TROUBLESHOOTING Troubleshooting

Explore 13 topics → USE CASES Use Cases

Separate client repositories, review paths, and audit records for agency delivery.

docs.title: Agent Prompt Guidelines
docs.text:
MVP Agents Agent Prompt Guidelines

Write role prompts that are specific, reviewable, and compatible with daemon verification.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Agent Prompt Guidelines covers prompt design inside Trey, Grovya Labs' local-first AI engineering operations cockpit. It explains how the console, Spring Boot control plane, Go daemon, git worktrees, PostgreSQL records, runtime adapters, and human approval process work together for this area.

[!NOTE] MVP status This page describes MVP behavior. Some integrations around it may still be phased, but the operating model is intended for the first customer-facing Trey workflows.

docs.title: When to Use It
docs.text:
Use this page when you are designing, operating, or reviewing prompt design. It is especially useful when a founder, developer, reviewer, or DevOps user needs to understand what Trey should automate, what the daemon must verify, and where a human decision is required.

  • Before configuring a task or workspace policy.
  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats prompt design as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Important Fields and Configuration
docs.text:
Most prompt design workflows depend on a small set of records that should remain visible in the console and audit trail.

  • workspaceId
  • repositoryId
  • taskId
  • runId
  • status
  • createdBy
  • createdAt

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
workspace: grovya-labs
repository: example-service
agent: Agent Prompt Guidelines
runner: local-macbook
runtime: codex-cli
approvalRequired: true

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy prompt design workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For prompt design, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.title: Agents
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Agents

Logical engineering roles with scope, prompts, permissions, and default review expectations.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Trey uses specialized logical Agent Roles rather than a single chat instance. Each agent role represents a technical specialization with distinct capabilities, prompts, and default review policies.

  • Trey Lead Agent: Coordinates task refinement, decomposes complex requirements, and reviews diffs before final verification.
  • Backend Agent: Focuses on backend APIs, Postgres schemas, database migrations, and unit tests.
  • Frontend Agent: Specializes in React UI layouts, state management, design tokens, and user experience flows.
  • PR Review Agent: An independent reviewer role that inspects code changes, risk assessments, and test coverage.

docs.title: Scope and Boundaries
docs.text:
Agents suggest and draft files, but they are not system administrators. They operate inside a strict sandbox and cannot bypass security limits.

  • No Direct Write: Agents write changes in local git worktrees. They have no push access to protected git branches.
  • Prompt Hardening: Agent system prompts strictly enforce policy compliance, secure coding practices, and tool usage regulations.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats integrations as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy integrations workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:

docs.title: Auth apis
docs.text:
Reference API Reference Auth APIs

Authenticate users, manage sessions, and authorize CLI connections.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Auth APIs manage identity validation, cookie rotation, active sessions, and CLI authorize flows.

  • Session Tokens: Gated by HttpOnly, Secure cookies in BFF context to defend against token theft.
  • CLI Token handshakes: Authenticates local command-line client sessions safely using PKCE-like authorization redirections.

docs.title: Auth Endpoints
docs.text:
POST /api/v1/auth/login        # Credentials login
POST /api/v1/auth/logout       # Revoke session
POST /api/v1/auth/refresh      # Cookie token rotation
GET  /api/v1/auth/sessions     # List active sessions
POST /api/v1/auth/cli/authorize # CLI OAuth handshake

  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats PR endpoints as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Important Fields and Configuration
docs.text:
Most PR endpoints workflows depend on a small set of records that should remain visible in the console and audit trail.

  • workspaceId
  • repositoryId
  • taskId
  • runId
  • status
  • createdBy
  • createdAt

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
GET /api/v1/pull/request
Authorization: Bearer <workspace-token>

HTTP 200
{
  "status": "Reference",
  "scope": "PR endpoints"
}

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy PR endpoints workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For PR endpoints, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.title: Security Architecture
docs.text:
MVP Architecture Security Architecture

Isolation, token boundaries, command audit, protected paths, secret scanning, and approval gates.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Trey uses a robust multi-layered security architecture spanning secure BFF endpoints, HTTP headers hardening, and sandboxed runner containers.

[!CAUTION] Zero Trust Control Plane The Spring Boot control plane assumes zero trust from runtimes and browser clients. No shell commands are executed on the backend orchestrator.

docs.title: Security Controls
docs.text:
1. BFF Cookie Isolation: All frontend tokens are converted to HttpOnly, SameSite, Secure cookies.
  2. Hardened Headers: Enforces CSP (Content Security Policy), HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security), frame-ancestors, and X-Content-Type-Options.
  3. Outbound Runner Daemon: Go runners connect outbound, requiring no inbound network routes.
  4. Scoped Role Gatekeepers: All API requests undergo cross-company and cross-workspace access validation.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Runtime Adapter Architecture
  • Integration Layer

docs.text:

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
workspace: grovya-labs
repository: example-service
agent: Integrations Screen
runner: local-macbook
runtime: codex-cli
approvalRequired: true

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy integration UI workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For integration UI, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.title: How It Works

docs.title: Platform Console
docs.text:
MVP Console UI Platform Console

The platform view for super admins to manage companies, users, subscriptions, runners, and complaints.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Platform Console is the administration control cockpit reserved for Super Admins. It manages billing subscriptions, resolves company accounts, audits logs, and handles troubleshooting tickets.

[!WARNING] Audit Enforcement Platform-level actions are recorded in an immutable ledger, requiring Super Admins to log a valid business reason for sensitive operations.

docs.title: Super Admin Operations
docs.text:
Super Admins supervise the global health of the Trey network without bypassing individual workspace approvals.

  • Company Lockout: Suspend or reactivate company workspace access based on billing or violations.
  • Support & Troubleshooting Sessions: Securely inspect runner logs and task states to debug client complaints.
  • Fleet Diagnostics: Quarantine failing runners and inspect global telemetry metrics.

docs.title: Run Console
docs.text:
MVP Console UI Run Console

Watch live agent output, daemon logs, telemetry, changed files, diff proposal, and approval state.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The run console is the live operating room for a task run. It brings together agent terminal output, daemon logs, runtime status, runner telemetry, changed files, diff proposal, approval panel, and event timeline in one page.

docs.title: Verified State vs Model Claims
docs.text:
The console must visualize daemon-verified state, not just what the model says it did. If a runtime says tests passed but the daemon did not observe a successful test command, Trey should show the check as missing or failed. If a runtime says it changed one file but git reports five, the git-derived list wins.

[!CAUTION] Daemon facts first Use runtime output for context, but use daemon observations for approvals: git diff, command exits, changed files, protected path checks, secret scan, and artifact records.

docs.title: Panels
docs.text:
A complete run console gives reviewers enough evidence to decide without opening several tools.

  • Live agent terminal for normalized runtime output.
  • Daemon log tail for commands, worktree actions, and verification.
  • Runtime status for adapter, auth, selected model, and process state.
  • Runner telemetry for CPU, memory, disk, queue, and active task.
  • Changed files and diff proposal from git.
  • Approval panel with risk, gates, and next privileged action.
  • Event timeline for replayable history.

docs.title: Example Event Flow
docs.text: The run console should make the sequence easy to audit from queue claim to approval.

docs.title: Run console event stream
docs.text: queued -> runner.claimed -> worktree.created -> runtime.started -> file.changed -> command.started -> command.finished -> diff.generated -> gates.passed -> approval.requested

docs.title: Agents
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Agents

Logical engineering roles with scope, prompts, permissions, and default review expectations.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Trey uses specialized logical Agent Roles rather than a single chat instance. Each agent role represents a technical specialization with distinct capabilities, prompts, and default review policies.

  • Trey Lead Agent: Coordinates task refinement, decomposes complex requirements, and reviews diffs before final verification.
  • Backend Agent: Focuses on backend APIs, Postgres schemas, database migrations, and unit tests.
  • Frontend Agent: Specializes in React UI layouts, state management, design tokens, and user experience flows.
  • PR Review Agent: An independent reviewer role that inspects code changes, risk assessments, and test coverage.

docs.title: Scope and Boundaries
docs.text:
Agents suggest and draft files, but they are not system administrators. They operate inside a strict sandbox and cannot bypass security limits.

  • No Direct Write: Agents write changes in local git worktrees. They have no push access to protected git branches.
  • Prompt Hardening: Agent system prompts strictly enforce policy compliance, secure coding practices, and tool usage regulations.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Repositories
  • Task

docs.title: Approvals
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Approvals

Human review checkpoints before Trey commits, pushes, opens PRs, merges, or deploys.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Approvals are the core guardrails in Trey. Our approval-first design ensures no modifications cross from private runner workspaces into shared codebases without human validation.

[!WARNING] Explicit Gated Actions Commit, branch push, pull request generation, and deployment tasks are paused at explicit approval checkpoints.

docs.title: Audit Trails & Evidence Collection
docs.text:
Every approval request compiles comprehensive evidence so reviewers can make informed, secure decisions.

  • Git Diffs: The precise git-derived patch proposed by the agent.
  • Command Log Trace: Real-time execution logs and terminal output from build/test commands.
  • Review Metadata: Logs of the decision maker (human actor), approval timestamp, and decision reason.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Task Runs
  • Git Worktrees

docs.title: Audit Trails
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Audit Trails

A durable history of who requested work, what ran, what changed, and who approved it.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Audit Trails covers governance history inside Trey, Grovya Labs' local-first AI engineering operations cockpit. It explains how the console, Spring Boot control plane, Go daemon, git worktrees, PostgreSQL records, runtime adapters, and human approval process work together for this area.

[!NOTE] MVP status This page describes MVP behavior. Some integrations around it may still be phased, but the operating model is intended for the first customer-facing Trey workflows.

docs.title: When to Use It
docs.text:
Use this page when you are designing, operating, or reviewing governance history. It is especially useful when a founder, developer, reviewer, or DevOps user needs to understand what Trey should automate, what the daemon must verify, and where a human decision is required.

  • Before configuring a task or workspace policy.
  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats governance history as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Important Fields and Configuration
docs.text:
Most governance history workflows depend on a small set of records that should remain visible in the console and audit trail.

  • workspaceId
  • repositoryId
  • taskId
  • runId
  • status
  • createdBy
  • createdAt

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
workspace: grovya-labs
repository: example-service
agent: Audit Trails
runner: local-macbook
runtime: codex-cli
approvalRequired: true

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy governance history workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For governance history, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Telemetry
  • Architecture Overview

docs.title: Daemon vs Agent
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Daemon vs Agent

Separate logical AI roles from the Go daemon process that executes and verifies work.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Trey separates the people-shaped concepts from the machine-shaped concepts. An agent is a logical role, such as Backend Agent or PR Review Agent. A daemon is the long-running Go process installed on a runner machine. A runtime is the coding tool the daemon starts, such as Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor CLI, or OpenCode/Ollama. A runner is the actual machine doing the work. A human is the final approver for actions that affect shared history or production systems.

  • Agent: logical role, prompt, scope, and permission policy.
  • Daemon: local executor that creates worktrees, starts runtimes, runs checks, and reports verified state.
  • Runtime: model or coding CLI used to edit code.
  • Runner: laptop, workstation, GPU server, or VM where the daemon runs.
  • Human: accountable approver for commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions.

docs.title: When to Use It
docs.text: Use this distinction whenever you configure a task, troubleshoot execution, or explain Trey to a teammate. Most workflow mistakes come from treating the model as the executor. In Trey, the runtime can suggest and edit, but the daemon verifies filesystem state and the human approves risk.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: A task selects an agent, repository, runner, and runtime. The control plane queues the task. The runner daemon claims it, creates an isolated worktree, launches the runtime with the agent prompt and task context, streams normalized events, calculates the git diff, runs checks, and asks for approval before publishing anything.

docs.title: Example assignment
docs.text:
agent: Backend Agent
runtime: Claude Code
runner: home-macbook
daemon: trey-daemon v0.1.x
repository: nivasaone-api
action: implement endpoint and wait for approval before PR

[!TIP] Concrete mental model Backend Agent + Claude Code runtime + home-macbook runner + Go daemon + nivasaone-api repo means the role decides how to work, Claude Code edits, the home MacBook executes, the daemon verifies, and the human approves.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text: Do not grant a runtime direct authority to commit or push outside the daemon path. Trey should trust git, process exits, checks, protected path rules, and approvals more than natural-language model claims.

docs.text:

docs.title: Git Worktrees
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Git Worktrees

How Trey isolates task execution from your main working copy and protected branches.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Trey isolates task execution from the developer's primary working directory by using Git Worktrees. This prevents uncommitted edits from being overwritten or combined with automated changes.

[!CAUTION] Sandbox Worktrees For each task claim, the runner daemon checks out a separate directory as a worktree, keeping your main branch clean and allowing parallel executions.

docs.title: Worktree Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. On task claim, daemon creates a task-scoped branch (e.g., trey/task-123).
  2. Git worktree is created in a isolated directory (e.g., <runner-temp>/worktrees/task-123).
  3. Runtimes modify files only in this worktree directory.
  4. Verification runs build/test in this path.
  5. Worktree is pruned once the task is completed or aborted.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Approvals
  • Events and Logs

docs.title: Repositories
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Repositories

Registered codebases Trey can clone, inspect, isolate, test, and propose changes against.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Repositories represent the codebases registered under a Trey workspace. Trey integrates with git hosting providers (GitHub, GitLab) to orchestrate work branches, fetch requirements, propose diffs, and generate pull requests.

[!CAUTION] Secure Access Keys Repository credentials (git tokens, SSH keys) are kept in secure control plane boundaries or local runner daemons. Agent runtimes are never given direct access to global credentials.

docs.title: Repository-Level Governance Policy
docs.text:
Each repository can define granular rules to guide agents and restrict automated changes.

  • AGENTS.md: Local guidelines containing architectural instructions, coding standards, and source-of-truth rules that agents must read before writing code.
  • .traiamb/repo.json: Technical configuration defining allowed commands, build/test scripts, and custom path classifications.

docs.title: repo.json configuration
docs.text:
{
  "build": "npm run build",
  "test": "npm run test",
  "allowlist": ["npm", "git", "vitest"],
  "protectedPaths": ["next.config.ts", "src/auth/**"]
}

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Workspaces
  • Agents

docs.title: Runners
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Runners

Machines that run the Trey daemon and perform isolated engineering work.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Runners represent the execution machines that host the Go runner daemon (trey-daemon). Runners poll the Spring Boot orchestrator for tasks, run them in sandboxed environments, and report health and telemetry.

[!NOTE] Outbound-Only Connection Runners establish outbound HTTP/WebSocket connections. They require no inbound ports open, making them compatible with home laptops and private office networks.

docs.title: Runner Architecture and Security
docs.text:
Runners are hardened execution environments designed to run code modifications safely.

  • Multi-Tenant Scoping: Runners belong to a specific workspace or company. Heartbeats are signed with cryptographically hashed runner tokens.
  • Liveness & Heartbeats: Runners report heartbeat updates every 10 seconds. Offline states are immediately flagged.
  • Telemetry Capture: Reports CPU load, memory utilization, disk space, and command execution queues.

docs.title: Daemon CLI telemetry check
docs.text:
trey-daemon status
trey-daemon telemetry --once

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Daemon vs Agent
  • Runtimes

docs.title: Runtime Adapters
docs.text:
Planned Core Concepts Runtime Adapters

The adapter contract that lets Trey normalize different coding runtimes into one event model.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Runtime adapters let Trey support multiple coding tools without tying the product to one model provider. The adapter contract is responsible for probing local availability, starting a session, stopping a session, and normalizing output into RuntimeEvent records that the daemon and console can understand.

docs.title: Why Trey Must Not Hardcode Ollama Only
docs.text: Teams will use different tools for different work: local OpenCode/Ollama for private experiments, Claude Code for complex product work, Codex CLI for repository-aware coding, Cursor CLI for editor-aligned workflows, and raw Ollama for future low-level model tasks. A stable adapter layer keeps Trey focused on orchestration, verification, approval, and audit rather than provider-specific behavior.

docs.title: Adapter Contract
docs.text: Each adapter exposes probe, start, stop, and event normalization. Probe reports installation and auth state. Start launches work in a daemon-owned worktree. Stop terminates a task cleanly. RuntimeEvent normalization turns provider-specific stdout, JSON, command messages, and errors into a shared stream.

docs.title: Pseudo interface
docs.text:
type RuntimeAdapter = {
  probe(context: ProbeContext): Promise<RuntimeProbe>;
  start(session: RuntimeSession): AsyncIterable<RuntimeEvent>;
  stop(runId: string, reason: StopReason): Promise<void>;
};

type RuntimeEvent = {
  runId: string;
  type: "stdout" | "stderr" | "tool_call" | "file_hint" | "error" | "completed";
  message: string;
  observedAt: string;
};

[!CAUTION] Events are not proof Runtime events explain what the tool said or attempted. The daemon still verifies changed files, diffs, commands, and checks from the local environment.

docs.title: How Claude, Codex, Cursor, and OpenCode Fit
docs.text: Adapters can wrap command-line tools, local HTTP APIs, or future SDKs. The console should show a consistent run console regardless of provider: live output, runtime status, daemon log tail, changed files, checks, diff proposal, approval panel, and event timeline.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Runtime Adapters Overview
  • Runtime Adapter Architecture
  • Runtime Detection
  • Runtimes
  • Task Runs

docs.title: Runtimes
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Runtimes

Coding and model tools such as Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor CLI, and OpenCode/Ollama.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Runtimes are the actual coding engines (e.g. Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor, Ollama/OpenCode) started by the Go daemon to inspect, edit, and refactor code.

  • Multi-Runtime Support: Trey is designed to be runtime-agnostic, letting users choose optimal models or tools for each task.
  • Local Runtimes: Run on the user machine (Ollama, local CLI) keeping code completely private.
  • Cloud-backed CLIs: Run via remote providers (Claude Code API) for complex code synthesis.

docs.title: Probing and Status Control
docs.text:
The runner daemon periodically probes the host system to check the installation, version, and authentication state of registered runtimes.

  • AVAILABLE: The binary is detected and authenticated.
  • AUTH_REQUIRED: The binary exists but requires authorization (e.g., Claude login expired).
  • UNAVAILABLE: The binary is missing or not in the system PATH.

docs.title: Task
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Task

Understand task lifecycle, plan, creation, agent execution, and summarization.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A Task is the primary operational primitive in Trey, representing a bounded engineering objective to be resolved on a repository. It encapsulates requirement definitions, coordinates agent routing, manages runner/runtime assignments, holds execution logs, captures mechanical verification evidence, and coordinates human approval gates.

[!NOTE] Task Design Goal A task provides a secure, visible playground where AI coding runtimes can propose modifications, while the Go daemon acts as a trusted local gatekeeper and verification agent.

docs.title: Task Creation and Refinement
docs.text:
Tasks are initialized through the console UI or imported from external planning boards. Every task must undergo refinement before execution.

  • Manual Creation: Users specify the target workspace, repository, assigned agent role, designated runner machine, runtime adapter, and description.
  • Imported Tasks: Webhooks or polling sync issues from GitHub or Jira, linking external metadata to the Trey task.
  • Lead Agent Refinement: The Trey Lead Agent reads the description, inspects repo settings, consults AGENTS.md/repo.json policy, and generates an implementation plan.

docs.title: Task Execution Lifecycle
docs.text:
Tasks transition through a strict, auditable state machine monitored by the Spring Boot control plane and executed by the Go daemon.

  • QUEUED: The task is waiting in the workspace queue for its designated runner.
  • CLAIMED / RUNNING: An active runner claims the task, checks out an isolated git worktree on a unique branch, and spins up the agent runtime.
  • VERIFYING: The runtime finishes execution. The daemon takes over to run compile, test, lint, protected path, and secret checks.
  • WAITING FOR APPROVAL: The verification checks complete successfully. The proposed diff, log traces, and risk score are presented for human review.
  • COMPLETED: The reviewer approves. The daemon commits, pushes task branch, and triggers PR creation.
  • FAILED: Execution halts due to compilation errors, test failures, runner disconnect, or security policy violations.

docs.title: How the Agent Treats the Task
docs.text:
The agent runtime (e.g. Claude Code or OpenCode) does not execute directly on your production environment. It runs inside a sandboxed git worktree and is governed by strict rules.

  • Workspace Boundaries: The runner daemon isolates the task in a separate folder, protecting the main copy.
  • Prompt and Context Constraints: The control plane attaches role prompts and repo rules to guide agent decisions.
  • Tool Execution Logging: All command invocations and file modifications are captured as events in real-time.

docs.title: Task Model Schema
docs.text:
{
  "id": "uuid-7-task-id",
  "workspaceId": "uuid-workspace-id",
  "repoId": "uuid-repository-id",
  "assignedAgentId": "uuid-agent-id",
  "assignedRunnerId": "uuid-runner-id",
  "status": "WAITING_FOR_APPROVAL",
  "priority": "HIGH",
  "title": "Fix memory leak in background worker"
}

  • Execution Lifecycle

  • Agents
  • Daemon vs Agent

docs.title: Workspaces
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Workspaces

The team or organization boundary for repositories, agents, runners, and audit history.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A Workspace is the administrative and security boundary within Trey. It partitions users, repositories, agent roles, runner fleets, task queues, and audit history. It is the logical boundary where engineering teams collaborate safely.

[!NOTE] Workspace Scope All resources are scoped to a workspace. An API request or runner heartbeat from Workspace A can never read, modify, or schedule work inside Workspace B.

docs.title: Personal vs Company Workspaces
docs.text:
Workspaces scale from solo developers to enterprise engineering organizations.

  • Personal Workspaces: Scoped to a single INDIVIDUAL_OWNER. Designed for local-only, mesh networks, or personal workflows.
  • Company Workspaces: Nested under a Company customer boundary. Managed by COMPANY_OWNER and WORKSPACE_ADMIN roles, allowing team collaboration, shared runners, and unified review rules.
  • RBAC Enforcement: Scoped roles (Developer, Reviewer, Admin) are verified for every request inside the workspace context.

docs.title: Cloud VM Runner
docs.text:
Planned Daemon and Runners Cloud VM Runner

Use cloud compute for elastic execution while retaining approval policy.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Cloud VM Runner covers cloud execution inside Trey, Grovya Labs' local-first AI engineering operations cockpit. It explains how the console, Spring Boot control plane, Go daemon, git worktrees, PostgreSQL records, runtime adapters, and human approval process work together for this area.

[!IMPORTANT] Planned status This page describes planned product behavior. Treat it as implementation direction until the feature is shipped and enabled for your workspace.

docs.title: When to Use It
docs.text:
Use this page when you are designing, operating, or reviewing cloud execution. It is especially useful when a founder, developer, reviewer, or DevOps user needs to understand what Trey should automate, what the daemon must verify, and where a human decision is required.

  • Before configuring a task or workspace policy.
  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats cloud execution as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Important Fields and Configuration
docs.text:
Most cloud execution workflows depend on a small set of records that should remain visible in the console and audit trail.

  • runnerId
  • runnerName
  • labels
  • heartbeatAt
  • runtimeCapabilities
  • queueDepth
  • activeRunId

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
trey-daemon status
trey-daemon telemetry --once
trey-daemon logs --tail 100

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy cloud execution workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For cloud execution, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.text:
Use this page when you are designing, operating, or reviewing runner liveness. It is especially useful when a founder, developer, reviewer, or DevOps user needs to understand what Trey should automate, what the daemon must verify, and where a human decision is required.

  • Before configuring a task or workspace policy.
  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats runner liveness as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Important Fields and Configuration
docs.text:
Most runner liveness workflows depend on a small set of records that should remain visible in the console and audit trail.

  • runnerId
  • runnerName
  • labels
  • heartbeatAt
  • runtimeCapabilities
  • queueDepth
  • activeRunId

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
trey-daemon status
trey-daemon telemetry --once
trey-daemon logs --tail 100

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy runner liveness workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For runner liveness, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy terminology workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For terminology, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy GitHub integration workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For GitHub integration, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

  • Before configuring a task or workspace policy.
  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats GitHub issues as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Polling vs Webhooks
docs.text:
Planned Integrations Polling vs Webhooks

Choose a synchronization strategy based on network topology and security posture.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Polling vs Webhooks covers sync transport inside Trey, Grovya Labs' local-first AI engineering operations cockpit. It explains how the console, Spring Boot control plane, Go daemon, git worktrees, PostgreSQL records, runtime adapters, and human approval process work together for this area.

[!IMPORTANT] Planned status This page describes planned product behavior. Treat it as implementation direction until the feature is shipped and enabled for your workspace.

docs.title: When to Use It
docs.text:
Use this page when you are designing, operating, or reviewing sync transport. It is especially useful when a founder, developer, reviewer, or DevOps user needs to understand what Trey should automate, what the daemon must verify, and where a human decision is required.

  • Before configuring a task or workspace policy.
  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats sync transport as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Important Fields and Configuration
docs.text:
Most sync transport workflows depend on a small set of records that should remain visible in the console and audit trail.

  • workspaceId
  • repositoryId
  • taskId
  • runId
  • status
  • createdBy
  • createdAt

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
workspace: grovya-labs
repository: example-service
agent: Polling vs Webhooks
runner: local-macbook
runtime: codex-cli
approvalRequired: true

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy sync transport workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For sync transport, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

Use this page when you are designing, operating, or reviewing Jira sync phase. It is especially useful when a founder, developer, reviewer, or DevOps user needs to understand what Trey should automate, what the daemon must verify, and where a human decision is required.

  • Before configuring a task or workspace policy.
  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats Jira sync phase as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Important Fields and Configuration
docs.text:
Most Jira sync phase workflows depend on a small set of records that should remain visible in the console and audit trail.

  • integrationId
  • provider
  • externalId
  • syncDirection
  • lastSyncAt
  • auditStatus

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
integration: jira
project: ENG
importStatus: Ready for Dev
syncAfterApproval: true

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy Jira sync phase workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For Jira sync phase, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.title: Runners
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Runners

Machines that run the Trey daemon and perform isolated engineering work.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Runners represent the execution machines that host the Go runner daemon (trey-daemon). Runners poll the Spring Boot orchestrator for tasks, run them in sandboxed environments, and report health and telemetry.

[!NOTE] Outbound-Only Connection Runners establish outbound HTTP/WebSocket connections. They require no inbound ports open, making them compatible with home laptops and private office networks.

docs.title: Runner Architecture and Security
docs.text:
Runners are hardened execution environments designed to run code modifications safely.

  • Multi-Tenant Scoping: Runners belong to a specific workspace or company. Heartbeats are signed with cryptographically hashed runner tokens.
  • Liveness & Heartbeats: Runners report heartbeat updates every 10 seconds. Offline states are immediately flagged.
  • Telemetry Capture: Reports CPU load, memory utilization, disk space, and command execution queues.

docs.title: Daemon CLI telemetry check
docs.text:
trey-daemon status
trey-daemon telemetry --once

docs.text:

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Cursor CLI Runtime covers Cursor CLI inside Trey, Grovya Labs' local-first AI engineering operations cockpit. It explains how the console, Spring Boot control plane, Go daemon, git worktrees, PostgreSQL records, runtime adapters, and human approval process work together for this area.

[!IMPORTANT] Planned status This page describes planned product behavior. Treat it as implementation direction until the feature is shipped and enabled for your workspace.

docs.title: When to Use It
docs.text:
Use this page when you are designing, operating, or reviewing Cursor CLI. It is especially useful when a founder, developer, reviewer, or DevOps user needs to understand what Trey should automate, what the daemon must verify, and where a human decision is required.

  • Before configuring a task or workspace policy.
  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats Cursor CLI as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Important Fields and Configuration
docs.text:
Most Cursor CLI workflows depend on a small set of records that should remain visible in the console and audit trail.

  • runtimeKey
  • adapterName
  • detectedVersion
  • authStatus
  • capabilities
  • fallbackPriority

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
workspace: grovya-labs
repository: example-service
agent: Cursor CLI Runtime
runner: local-macbook
runtime: codex-cli
approvalRequired: true

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy Cursor CLI workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For Cursor CLI, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.text:
• Codex CLI Runtime
  • Raw Ollama API Runtime

docs.title: Runtimes
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Runtimes

Coding and model tools such as Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor CLI, and OpenCode/Ollama.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Runtimes are the actual coding engines (e.g. Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor, Ollama/OpenCode) started by the Go daemon to inspect, edit, and refactor code.

  • Multi-Runtime Support: Trey is designed to be runtime-agnostic, letting users choose optimal models or tools for each task.
  • Local Runtimes: Run on the user machine (Ollama, local CLI) keeping code completely private.
  • Cloud-backed CLIs: Run via remote providers (Claude Code API) for complex code synthesis.

docs.title: Probing and Status Control
docs.text:
The runner daemon periodically probes the host system to check the installation, version, and authentication state of registered runtimes.

  • AVAILABLE: The binary is detected and authenticated.
  • AUTH_REQUIRED: The binary exists but requires authorization (e.g., Claude login expired).
  • UNAVAILABLE: The binary is missing or not in the system PATH.

docs.title: Active Sessions
docs.text:
MVP Safety and Governance Active Sessions

Manage and audit active CLI and web console sessions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Active Sessions protect the platform from unauthorized token reuse. Trey tracks all active user logins, CLI handshakes, and API tokens under a single audit dashboard.

[!CAUTION] Session Limits Trey enforces session limits based on user role and client type (e.g., maximum 1 web console session and 2 CLI sessions for Developers) to prevent account sharing and token leakage.

docs.title: Audit and Revocation Flow
docs.text:
Users can audit active sessions directly from the Settings screen inside the console.

  • Session details: Shows client type (Web, CLI, Desktop, Mobile), Device ID, IP Address, and Last Active timestamp.
  • Immediate Revocation: Revoking a session destroys its token mapping on the control plane. Subsequent requests are rejected, and the device is logged out.

docs.title: Session management endpoints
docs.text:
GET /api/v1/auth/sessions
DELETE /api/v1/auth/sessions/{sessionId}/revoke

docs.title: Approval Gates
docs.text:
MVP Safety and Governance Approval Gates

Require human approval before commit, push, PR, merge, deployment, or sensitive command execution.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Approval Gates covers approval policy inside Trey, Grovya Labs' local-first AI engineering operations cockpit. It explains how the console, Spring Boot control plane, Go daemon, git worktrees, PostgreSQL records, runtime adapters, and human approval process work together for this area.

[!NOTE] MVP status This page describes MVP behavior. Some integrations around it may still be phased, but the operating model is intended for the first customer-facing Trey workflows.

docs.title: When to Use It
docs.text:
Use this page when you are designing, operating, or reviewing approval policy. It is especially useful when a founder, developer, reviewer, or DevOps user needs to understand what Trey should automate, what the daemon must verify, and where a human decision is required.

  • Before configuring a task or workspace policy.
  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats approval policy as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Important Fields and Configuration
docs.text:
Most approval policy workflows depend on a small set of records that should remain visible in the console and audit trail.

  • approvalId
  • requestedAction
  • riskLevel
  • requiredReviewer
  • decision
  • decidedAt

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
workspace: grovya-labs
repository: example-service
agent: Approval Gates
runner: local-macbook
runtime: codex-cli
approvalRequired: true

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy approval policy workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For approval policy, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.title: Safety Model
docs.text:
MVP Safety and Governance Safety Model

Agent writes code, daemon verifies facts, console visualizes state, and humans approve risk.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Trey uses a layered safety model: the agent writes code, the daemon observes and verifies, the console visualizes verified state, and the human approves risk. No commit, push, pull request, merge, or deployment should happen without an explicit approval record.

docs.title: Source of Truth
docs.text:
Git is the source of truth for changed files and diffs. Build, test, and lint checks are daemon-owned. Protected path rules and secret scans are evaluated before approval. Runtime messages are useful context but never sufficient proof.

  • Agent proposes and edits.
  • Daemon creates worktree and verifies the filesystem.
  • Console displays git-derived state and check results.
  • Human approves or requests changes.
  • Control plane records every privileged transition.

docs.title: Approval Boundaries
docs.text: Approval gates should exist anywhere Trey crosses from private work into shared systems: commit, push, PR creation, merge, deployment, protected path edit, and sensitive command execution.

docs.title: Policy sketch
docs.text:
approval:
  requiredFor:
    - commit
    - push
    - pull_request
    - protected_path
    - deployment
  blockOn:
    - secret_detected
    - tests_failed
    - direct_main_edit

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
Do not let external integrations, model output, or convenience buttons bypass the safety model. GitHub and Jira can request or reflect work, but Trey should remain the execution and approval record.

[!WARNING] No invisible publishing A user should always be able to answer who approved a change, what diff was approved, which checks ran, and what external action followed.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Deployments Screen
  • Approval Gates

docs.title: Token Isolation
docs.text:
MVP Safety and Governance Token Isolation

Separate control-plane credentials, runtime credentials, Git tokens, and runner tokens.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Token Isolation limits the damage of token compromise by partitioning credentials based on entity and scope.

  • User Tokens: Compact JWTs containing identity pointers (sid) stored inside BFF cookies.
  • Runner Tokens: Cryptographically hashed runner keys scoped strictly to fetch, claim, and log task executions within their designated workspace.
  • Git Provider Tokens: Scoped oauth keys used by the daemon to publish branches and open PRs.

[!CAUTION] Isolation Principle If a runner token is compromised, the attacker can only access that specific workspace's queues and cannot impersonate users or read other company workspaces.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• No Direct Main Edits
  • Active Sessions

docs.title: Task
docs.text:
MVP Core Concepts Task

Understand task lifecycle, plan, creation, agent execution, and summarization.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A Task is the primary operational primitive in Trey, representing a bounded engineering objective to be resolved on a repository. It encapsulates requirement definitions, coordinates agent routing, manages runner/runtime assignments, holds execution logs, captures mechanical verification evidence, and coordinates human approval gates.

[!NOTE] Task Design Goal A task provides a secure, visible playground where AI coding runtimes can propose modifications, while the Go daemon acts as a trusted local gatekeeper and verification agent.

docs.title: Task Creation and Refinement
docs.text:
Tasks are initialized through the console UI or imported from external planning boards. Every task must undergo refinement before execution.

  • Manual Creation: Users specify the target workspace, repository, assigned agent role, designated runner machine, runtime adapter, and description.
  • Imported Tasks: Webhooks or polling sync issues from GitHub or Jira, linking external metadata to the Trey task.
  • Lead Agent Refinement: The Trey Lead Agent reads the description, inspects repo settings, consults AGENTS.md/repo.json policy, and generates an implementation plan.

docs.title: Task Execution Lifecycle
docs.text:
Tasks transition through a strict, auditable state machine monitored by the Spring Boot control plane and executed by the Go daemon.

  • QUEUED: The task is waiting in the workspace queue for its designated runner.
  • CLAIMED / RUNNING: An active runner claims the task, checks out an isolated git worktree on a unique branch, and spins up the agent runtime.
  • VERIFYING: The runtime finishes execution. The daemon takes over to run compile, test, lint, protected path, and secret checks.
  • WAITING FOR APPROVAL: The verification checks complete successfully. The proposed diff, log traces, and risk score are presented for human review.
  • COMPLETED: The reviewer approves. The daemon commits, pushes task branch, and triggers PR creation.
  • FAILED: Execution halts due to compilation errors, test failures, runner disconnect, or security policy violations.

docs.title: How the Agent Treats the Task
docs.text:
The agent runtime (e.g. Claude Code or OpenCode) does not execute directly on your production environment. It runs inside a sandboxed git worktree and is governed by strict rules.

  • Workspace Boundaries: The runner daemon isolates the task in a separate folder, protecting the main copy.
  • Prompt and Context Constraints: The control plane attaches role prompts and repo rules to guide agent decisions.
  • Tool Execution Logging: All command invocations and file modifications are captured as events in real-time.

docs.title: Task Model Schema
docs.text:
{
  "id": "uuid-7-task-id",
  "workspaceId": "uuid-workspace-id",
  "repoId": "uuid-repository-id",
  "assignedAgentId": "uuid-agent-id",
  "assignedRunnerId": "uuid-runner-id",
  "status": "WAITING_FOR_APPROVAL",
  "priority": "HIGH",
  "title": "Fix memory leak in background worker"
}

docs.title: Execution Lifecycle
docs.text:
MVP Task Workflow Execution Lifecycle

How a task moves from queued to running, verifying, approval, completed, or failed.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Trey execution lifecycle represents the complete path a task takes from creation to PR submission. It is coordinates via a state machine between the Next.js console, Spring Boot control plane, and Go daemon.

  • Intake & Refinement: Task is created and refined by the Lead Agent.
  • Queue Allocation: Assigned to the queue of an online, matching runner.
  • Worktree Checkout: Daemon checks out worktree on a task-scoped branch.
  • Runtime Synthesizing: Agent runtime receives guidelines and performs the coding task.
  • Mechanical verification: Daemon executes build, test, and lint commands, verifying the output.
  • Risk Review & Approval: Diffs and logs are presented for human review.
  • Git Publication: Upon approval, daemon commits, pushes branch, and creates the PR.

docs.text:
MVP Task Workflow Manual Task Creation

Create work directly in Trey with enough context for an agent to execute safely.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Manual Task Creation covers task intake inside Trey, Grovya Labs' local-first AI engineering operations cockpit. It explains how the console, Spring Boot control plane, Go daemon, git worktrees, PostgreSQL records, runtime adapters, and human approval process work together for this area.

[!NOTE] MVP status This page describes MVP behavior. Some integrations around it may still be phased, but the operating model is intended for the first customer-facing Trey workflows.

docs.title: When to Use It
docs.text:
Use this page when you are designing, operating, or reviewing task intake. It is especially useful when a founder, developer, reviewer, or DevOps user needs to understand what Trey should automate, what the daemon must verify, and where a human decision is required.

  • Before configuring a task or workspace policy.
  • While reviewing a run that produced code, logs, checks, or artifacts.
  • When explaining the boundary between AI assistance and human accountability.
  • When troubleshooting a mismatch between runtime output and daemon-verified state.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Trey treats task intake as part of a controlled task lifecycle. The backend stores durable records, the console presents the workflow, the daemon performs local execution in an isolated worktree, and runtime adapters translate provider-specific behavior into normalized events. The result should be reviewable from the task page without trusting model text alone.

  • The control plane records intent, selected repository, selected agent, selected runner, selected runtime, and current state.
  • The daemon claims eligible work, prepares the worktree, starts the runtime, captures command output, and reports telemetry.
  • Verification uses git diff, changed files, command exits, build/test/lint results, protected path checks, and secret scanning.
  • Approval gates block commit, push, PR, merge, and deployment actions until a human approves the exact proposed result.

docs.title: Important Fields and Configuration
docs.text:
Most task intake workflows depend on a small set of records that should remain visible in the console and audit trail.

  • workspaceId
  • repositoryId
  • taskId
  • runId
  • status
  • createdBy
  • createdAt

docs.title: Configuration example
docs.text:
workspace: grovya-labs
repository: example-service
agent: Manual Task Creation
runner: local-macbook
runtime: codex-cli
approvalRequired: true

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy task intake workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For task intake, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

docs.text:
• Integration Layer
  • Repo and Agent Selection

docs.title: Operational Workflow
docs.text:
A healthy test diagnostics workflow is explicit and replayable. The user creates or imports work, chooses the repo and agent, selects a runner and runtime, watches live logs, reviews daemon-derived changes, and decides whether the next privileged action is allowed.

  • Create or import a task with clear acceptance criteria.
  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For test diagnostics, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.

  • Select a repository and agent that match the technical scope.
  • Select a runner that has repository access and the required runtime installed.
  • Let the daemon run checks and generate a diff from git.
  • Approve, reject, or request changes based on verified evidence.

docs.title: Safety Notes
docs.text:
For review workflow, Trey should prefer verified local state over convenience. Runtime output can be useful, but it is not a substitute for daemon-owned checks or explicit approval.

  • Never treat a runtime message as proof that files changed or tests passed.
  • Do not allow direct edits to protected branches from an agent session.
  • Block or escalate protected paths, detected secrets, failed checks, and unknown command execution.
  • Keep approval records tied to the exact diff, command results, actor, and timestamp.

[!CAUTION] Human approval remains the control point The daemon can automate execution and verification, but the accountable action is the human decision to publish or reject the proposed change.
